texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas permeability (Pi) and  diffusion (Di) coefficients in  respect to  several gases  (H2, 02,  N2,  CO, CO2, CH4) have  been measured  for poly(2,6-dimethylphenylene oxide) (PMPO), poly(2,6-diphenylphenylene oxide) (PPPO),  and  phenylene  oxide copolymers containing methyl,  phenyl,  and allyl radicals as side  groups.  X-ray diffraction study shows that  both homopolymers are  semicrystalline materials, whereas  all the  copolymers  are  completely amorphous.  The results how that a replacement of  methyl  by  phenyl groups  in  PMPO/PPPO pair is accompanied  by  a  decrease in  the P values. A transition from semicrystalline PMPO to  amorphous  copolymers  results in  a  decrease in  permeability and  solubility coefficients and  not  in  a growth  of  these parameters as  can  be  expected on  the basis of  the behavior of  other semicrystalline polymers (e.g. polyolefins). It is supposed  that the  crystallites of  PMPO,  and  possibly of  PPPO  are packed  loosely and, hence, take part in  sorption and  gas transport. This  assumption is in  agreement with  numerous  X-ray  data as  well as  the  results of  positron annihilation study  of these polymers. ©  1998  Elsevier Science B.V.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The vast majority of  results reported during the  last decade  on  transport properties of  polymeric materials for gas  separation membranes refers to  amorphous  and not  semicrystalline polymers  [ 1-4]. The  reason for it is related to  the  well-known  behavior of  semicrystalline materials, first described by Michaels  and Bixler [5] who showed that an  increase in  the  content of  crystalline  fraction in  polyethylene leads  to  a decrease  in permeability, diffusion, and solubility coefficients.
texts.text: The vast majority of  results reported during the  last decade  on  transport properties of  polymeric materials for gas  separation membranes refers to  amorphous  and not  semicrystalline polymers  [ 1-4]. The  reason for it is related to  the  well-known  behavior of  semicrystalline materials, first described by Michaels  and Bixler [5] who showed that an  increase in  the  content of  crystalline  fraction in  polyethylene leads  to  a decrease  in permeability, diffusion, and solubility coefficients.

texts.orig: Crystalline phases  acts as  impermeable  and  incapable for gas  sorption dispersions similar to  solid fillers and, therefore, their presence  in a material only makes diffusion paths  across  the  membrane  more tortuous and  long. As a  reduction in  permeability is  usually not accompanied  by simultaneous growth  in  permselectivity, the only motivation for  a search  for  highly crystalline polymers as membrane  materials could be their improved  mechanical  and,  sometimes, filmforming  properties.

texts.orig: characterized by relatively high  gas  permeability coefficients [6]. Another, and  a  more  interesting feature of  this material is that its amorphous  regions are  more dense  than the crystalline ones, which  is not  typical for semicrystalline polyolefins [7]. The  dimension of  void space  in  the  crystals have been estimated as large enough  so  as  to  accommodate  a  molecule like CO2  and yet  too  small  for a  molecule such  as  C3Hs  [8]. Further detailed studies indicated that, although gas  sorption and permeation apparently proceed  mainly  in  amorphous  regions, some contribution is  also  provided by the more  loosely  packed crystalline regions  [9]. Recently, these results were  confirmed by an investigation of  sorption in  and  dilation of  PMP  [10].

texts.orig: An  important difference between  PMP  and lower polyolefins is  that the  former undergoes  glass transition at  ca. 30°C,  whereas  the  glass-transition temperatures  of thoroughly  studied  semicrystalline polyethylene and  polypropylene are much  below 0°C.  Therefore, the  amorphous  phase  of  PMP in  the temperature  range of permeability  measurement (usually 20-35°C)  is  below or  just in  the  vicinity of glass transition. Because  of  this, crystalline r gions in PMP  are  surrounded by a fairly rigid glassy matrix, whereas those  of  e.g.  polyethylene by much  more flexible chains, the  mobility of  which  is only  partially limited by adjacent crystallites. This  can  be  an  additional peculiarity of  PMP as compared with lower polyolefins. On  the  other  hand,  a question can be raised  about  the  behavior  of other  semicrystalline polymers  having  glassy amorphous  phases  at  ambient temperatures.

texts.orig: Poly(2,6-dimethyl-l,4-phenylene oxide)  (PMPO) can  be  considered as  another interesting object of  this kind. Just as  PMP, this polymer  has  been  recognized as a membrane  material with fairly high permeation parameters  [11].  Later  on,  permeabilities of  PMPO and  the products of  its chemical modification, sorption by  and  dilation of  PMPO  at high  pressure was  reported [12-20]. It  is  amazing,  however,  that  in all  these papers  (beyond  a rare  exception [19])  the  authors did  not  note the  fact that PMPO can  have,  depending on a solvent used  or  conditions of  film  preparation, semicrystalline structure with  a  content of  crystalline phase  varying within a wide  range.
texts.text: Poly(2,6-dimethyl-l,4-phenylene oxide)  (PMPO) can  be  considered as  another interesting object of  this kind. Just as  PMP, this polymer  has  been  recognized as a membrane  material with fairly high permeation parameters  [11].  Later  on,  permeabilities of  PMPO and  the products of  its chemical modification, sorption by  and  dilation of  PMPO  at high  pressure was  reported [12-20]. It  is  amazing,  however,  that  in all  these papers  (beyond  a rare  exception [19])  the  authors did  not  note the  fact that PMPO can  have,  depending on a solvent used  or  conditions of  film  preparation, semicrystalline structure with  a  content of  crystalline phase  varying within a wide  range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Indeed, PMPO is  crystallizable polymer,  although its  crystallization from melts  is  complicated  by a narrow  temperature gap between  the  glass-transition temperature and melting point. On the  other hand,  it crystallizes asily from solutions (see  Ref.  [21]  and other references therein). Even single crystals can  be obtained by  slow  crystallization of  PMPO from  dilute solutions  in  c~-pinene [22],  although sometimes PMPO films prepared from  solutions exhibit no crystallinity [19]. Therefore, a question on phase  composition of PMPO films  and its  possible effects on transport properties deserves scrutiny.

On the  other hand,  it crystallizes asily from solutions (see  Ref.  [21]  and other references therein). Even single crystals can  be obtained by  slow  crystallization of  PMPO from  dilute solutions  in  c~-pinene [22],  although sometimes PMPO films prepared from  solutions exhibit no crystallinity [19]. Therefore, a question on phase  composition of PMPO films  and its  possible effects on transport properties deserves scrutiny.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  this paper, we report gas  permeation parameters for PMPO,  its structural analog poly(2,6-diphenylphenylene  oxide)  (PPPO) and random copolymers  consisting  of 2,6-dimethylphenylene oxide and  2.6diphenylphenylene oxide  or  2-methyl-6-allylphenylene oxide repeat  units. Phase composition  of all the samples was  checked using wide-angle  X-ray diffraction (WAXD) method.  Additionally, low-pressure  gas sorption isotherms  of PMPO, PPPO and copolymers  were obtained. The polymers  were also studied using  positron annihilation method.
texts.text: In  this paper, we report gas  permeation parameters for PMPO,  its structural analog poly(2,6-diphenylphenylene  oxide)  (PPPO) and random copolymers  consisting  of 2,6-dimethylphenylene oxide and  2.6diphenylphenylene oxide  or  2-methyl-6-allylphenylene oxide repeat  units. Phase composition  of all the samples was  checked using wide-angle  X-ray diffraction (WAXD) method.  Additionally, low-pressure  gas sorption isotherms  of PMPO, PPPO and copolymers  were obtained. The polymers  were also studied using  positron annihilation method.

texts.orig: 2.1. Polymers
texts.text: 2.1. Polymers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two  homopolymers (PMPO and PPPO)  and random  copolymers  containing 2,6-diphenylphenylene oxide  and  2,6-dimethylphenylene oxide units in  molecular  ratios 2.5/97.5 and 25/75 were prepared  via oxidative (co)polycondensation f  the  corresponding substituted phenols.  In the same  manner, poly(2methyl-6-allylphenylene oxide-co-2,6-dimethylphenylene oxide)  with the molecular  ratio  12.5/87.5 was  prepared. The  polymers were synthesized at The  Research Institute of Chemical Technology (Novosibirsk, Russia). According  to  the  manufacturer, the  purity of  the  polymers  was > 99.9%,  with  major impurities being  diphenoquinone and traces of  the copper  catalyst. The details of the procedure  and conditions of  polymerization can  be  found  elsewhere [23]. Viscosity average  molecular mass of  the  polymers was in  the  range  100  000-120  000.

texts.orig: 2.3.  Measurements
texts.text: 2.3.  Measurements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeability measurements  were  carried out  using chromatographic and  mass spectrometric instruments described in  [ 17,24]. In  the  chromatographic setup, the pressure  drop across  the membrane  was  not total, whereas  the  penetrant partial pressure drop  was about 0.2  MPa. Helium served as  a  gas  carrier. In  the  mass spectrometric apparatus, downstream pressure were kept  as  low as  1-0.1  Pa,  whereas  individual penetrant pressure in  the feed  part of  the  cell was  in the  2-40  kPa range. Sorption of  oxygen  and  nitrogen was measured using  DigiSorb  2600  and ASAP 2400 instruments (Micromeritics, USA), respectively.

A monotonic  growth of  crystalline phase  content shown for  both  powder samples  and  the  films indicates that some mobility of chains  can be observed  even  at  temperatures 200 K below the  glass-transition temperature, which seems to  be  rather unusual. On the  other hand,  although the WAXD patterns obtained by means of  the  two  instruments  (DRON-4  and  Rigaku)  are  similar, some peculiarities ofthe  peak  fitting algorithms in  both  cases are somewhat different. Hence,  we consider these  values as  only  semiquantitative and did  not  use  the  crystallinity found in  any equation or  correlation.

texts.orig: WAXD patterns obtained for  PMPO and PPPO  in this work and reported in  some other  studies [26,27] do not  differ significantly. For  example,  Weing et  al. [26]  presented, in  the  studies of  PMPO, a series of broad  peaks  superimposed over  amorphous  halo  for 20 lying in  the 5-25  °  range. The maximum  of  the  halo  is situated in  the  vicinity of 14-15  ° .  This  is  in  accord with  the  WAXD scan  shown in  Fig.  la. On the  other hand,  Aguilar-Vega and Paul  [19]  presented WAXD scan  with  the  peaks  less defined for  the  PMPO films prepared  by  casting  from chloroform. A  possible explanation of  this behavior can  be  related to  different conditions of  film  formation in  two works.  The films of  PMPO were  prepared, in  the  latter work,  by  a  more rapid evaporation of  chloroform (for 4 h at 50°C)  than in  the  present study, which  can  result in  a  smaller size of  the  crystallites.
texts.text: WAXD patterns obtained for  PMPO and PPPO  in this work and reported in  some other  studies [26,27] do not  differ significantly. For  example,  Weing et  al. [26]  presented, in  the  studies of  PMPO, a series of broad  peaks  superimposed over  amorphous  halo  for 20 lying in  the 5-25  °  range. The maximum  of  the  halo  is situated in  the  vicinity of 14-15  ° .  This  is  in  accord with  the  WAXD scan  shown in  Fig.  la. On the  other hand,  Aguilar-Vega and Paul  [19]  presented WAXD scan  with  the  peaks  less defined for  the  PMPO films prepared  by  casting  from chloroform. A  possible explanation of  this behavior can  be  related to  different conditions of  film  formation in  two works.  The films of  PMPO were  prepared, in  the  latter work,  by  a  more rapid evaporation of  chloroform (for 4 h at 50°C)  than in  the  present study, which  can  result in  a  smaller size of  the  crystallites.

texts.orig: A  feature of PMPO is  a very small  difference between its glass transition Tg and melting  Tm  temperatures. A  correlation exists between  Tg and Tm in numerous polymers  [28].  It was shown that  the  following  linear  equation holds  fairly  accurately: Tm=  aTg where a =  1.5-2.  For PMPO, according to  Warsidlo  [29], Tg = 480 K,  and  Tm -535 K,  therefore a =  1.1. The corresponding ratio for PPPO, equal to 1.5 [28],  is  in agreement with the correlation mentioned  above.  A  question can  be raised whether this is  caused  by  too  high  Tg or  too  low Tm values of PMPO.  In  order  to  answer  it, one can  use  the  group contribution method for prediction of  the  glass-transition  temperatures with  the  increments given  by Van Krevelen  [28]. This  estimate gives  the  Tg value  for PMPO to  be  483 K, which is  in  excellent agreement with  experimental value. For PPPO,  the  group  contribution method  gives  the value 500 K, again  in accordance  with the experimental  measurements. Therefore, it can  be  concluded  that some peculiarities of  the  crystalline lattice of  PMPO allows it to  melt  at least 180 K  below the  anticipated melting  temperature.

texts.orig: The effect of  a  chemical  structure of  side groups  in phenylene oxide  polymers can  be  studied using homopolymers  PMPO and  PPPO  as  examples. The crystallinity  of  the  samples  of  these polymers  was approximately the  same,  therefore, a phase  composition of  the  films should not  have  masked  the  structural effects. The permeability (P), diffusion (D)  and  solubility (S)  coefficients for  several gases  measured  by mass spectrometric method are  presented in  Table  2. Diffusion coefficient were  determined using  the  timelag  method,  whereas  the S  values were  estimated from the  equation S = P/D.

texts.orig: As discussed earlier, the  gas  permeation parameters of  PMPO have been reported in many  papers  [1114,16-18].  There is some  scatter in the P  values observed. For example,  permeability coefficients for CO2  vary  threefold, whereas  those  for  Nz show much better agreement. A  possible explanation of  this scatter due  to  the  influences of  different crystallinity of  the samples  studied in  different works,  does  not  seem to be very  convincing bearing  in  mind similar values  of the transport parameters  of amorphous PMPO and PPPO samples [19]  and crystalline films  studied in this work (see Table  2  and  discussion below). It is seen that  polymer  samples  containing ca.  50%  of  crystalline  phase are characterized by approximately the same  permeability coefficients as fully  amorphous samples.

texts.orig: Permeability  and  diffusivity of  semicrystalline PMPO and PPPO  films  studied in  the  present work at  295 K  should  be compared with  those  reported by Aguilar-Vega and  Paul  [ 19]  for  completely amorphous samples  of  the  same polymers  at  308 K. In  order  to account  for  differences in  the  temperatures of  measurements, the  original values  reported in [19]  were recalculated to  295 K using  the  activation energies Ep and ED given  in  this paper. As is  seen  from  Table  2,  a very  good coincidence is  observed  for  the  P  and D values found  for  amorphous and semicrystalline polymers.  This  unexpected result can  be interpreted as  an indication that  effects of crystallinity on transport parameters in  phenylene  oxide  polymers  are  entirely different from those  observed  for  lower  semicrystalline  polyolefins.

texts.orig: Several other  qualitative observations made in  the studies of  amorphous and semicrystalline PMPO and PPPO  should  also be  noted. In  both  cases, replacement of methyl by phenyl groups resulted in a marked decrease  in  permeability. A  distinction between the behavior of  amorphous and semicrystalline polymers is  that, in  the  latter case, the  diffusion coefficients of different gases  are  hardly changed  due  to  this replacement and  all the  variation of  the  P values is  seemingly induced  by solubility, whereas  permeability of  amorphous  polymers  changes  due  to  variation of  both  the  D and  S values. It is  also noteworthy  that, in  both  cases, the minimum effect  of this  structural variation is observed  for  carbon  dioxide.

texts.orig: In  conclusion, the  observations on permeability of phenylene  oxide  polymers  having  different crystallinity can  be  interpreted assuming  that local transport parameters of  the  crystalline and  amorphous  phases of these  materials do not  differ as  strongly as  in  corresponding  phases  of  polyethylene or  other lower  polyolefins. Although  the  density of  crystalline PMPO (in contrast to  PMP)  is  higher than  that of  its amorphous phase [28],  there  are  a number of  indications that crystalline lattice of PMPO is packed relatively loosely. Thus,  it is  known  [31]  that PMPO is  capable of  forming complexes  with CH2C12  or CH2Br2, which contains two  moles  of  these solvents per  repeat unit of the  polymer. It is amazing  that the  X-ray  pattern of  the complex is  identical to  the  one of  original polymer, indicating no  loss  of original structure after  the formation  of  the  complex.  These complexes  easily decay under vacuum, hence no strong  bonds were involved in  these complex  formation, and  they can  be considered as  inclusion  compounds.  Methylene chloride molecules  can  occupy  the  periodic array  of voids  such  as  occur  in  a crystal composed of  helical molecules. Indeed,  a helical conformation was confirmed  in X-ray study  of another  phenylene  oxide polymer  - PPPO  [32].  It  should  be recalled that the crystal lattice of  PMP also  has a helical structure. Indications have  been obtained that  even  larger solvent  molecules  (the  homologous series from toluene to n-hexylbenzene) can be  incorporated into the crystal lattice of PMPO, probably  stabilizing helix formation [33].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas sorption isotherms were  measured  at  293 K and subatmospheric pressure for  PMPO, PPPO  and their copolymers.  The  solubility coefficients were calculated from linear isotherms, thereafter diffusion coefficients were found  from  the  equation D  = P/S, where the  P values  had been determined by means of  chromatographic  method. The  S  values  found by  the sorption (Table  4)  and permeation methods (Table  1) for  the  homopolymers are  in  reasonable agreement. The  data given in Table  4, as well as the results presented in  Table 1  for  semicrystalline homopolymers,  indicate that  the chemical structure of side groups in phenylene oxide polymers exerts much stronger  effects  on  solubility coefficients rather than  diffusivity. The diffusion coefficients of the  homopolymers obtained by the  time-lag method and via  solubility coefficients do not differ significantly.

texts.orig: Positron annihilation lifetime (PAL) spectroscopy is a  powerful  method  for characterization of free volume in  polymers.  PAL  spectra consist of  several components: times 7-i  and corresponding statistical weights  or intensities /~.  Usually  the  best  statistical fit can be obtained for  three-component spectra; the longer times  7-3  being  a  characteristics of  free volume. Indeed, various correlations have  been  reported between 7-3, 13,  their product  and such quantities as diffusion coefficients, parameters  of sorption  isotherms,  which  should be sensitive to free  volume and  free-volume  size distribution [25,37-39].  On the  other  hand,  an average  size  of  free-volume elements  can  be  estimated using  different  model approaches  [40-43].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus,  the  study  of  the  transport and sorption properties  and  free  volume  in phenylene oxide polymers indicated  that  phase composition  of  these  materials affects their properties (the  P and S values, as  well  as PAL  spectra  sensitive to  free  volume) to  a much  less extent than  their chemical  composition. In  this regard, the  behavior  of  phenylene  oxide  polymers  differ substantially from  that of lower polyolefins or guttapercha, where  the P, D,  and  S  values  are directly proportional to  the  amorphous  phase content. It  can be  assumed  that crystallites in PMPO and  PPPO contribute noticeably in  the  gas  sorption and transport processes. This  point of  view  is  strongly supported by findings of X-ray studies of these  polymers:  their ability to  co-crystallize with  bulky  organic  solvents and  formation of  inclusion compounds with  molecules like methylene  dichloride [31-33].
texts.text: Thus,  the  study  of  the  transport and sorption properties  and  free  volume  in phenylene oxide polymers indicated  that  phase composition  of  these  materials affects their properties (the  P and S values, as  well  as PAL  spectra  sensitive to  free  volume) to  a much  less extent than  their chemical  composition. In  this regard, the  behavior  of  phenylene  oxide  polymers  differ substantially from  that of lower polyolefins or guttapercha, where  the P, D,  and  S  values  are directly proportional to  the  amorphous  phase content. It  can be  assumed  that crystallites in PMPO and  PPPO contribute noticeably in  the  gas  sorption and transport processes. This  point of  view  is  strongly supported by findings of X-ray studies of these  polymers:  their ability to  co-crystallize with  bulky  organic  solvents and  formation of  inclusion compounds with  molecules like methylene  dichloride [31-33].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A question, which  remains  still unanswered  is, why films of  different phase  composition, as  revealed by WAXD scans, are  sometimes  obtained in  rather similar conditions of  film  casting (see  e.g. Aguilar-Vega and  Paul  [19]  and  this work).  It can  be  supposed  that crystalline particles of  different sizes can  be  formed depending of temperature and, hence,  the  rate  of solvent evaporation. If  the  size  of  these  crystallites is  less  than  some limiting value, WAXD scans  can appear  as  those of  a  completely amorphous  material. Indeed, Wenig et  al. [26]  showed  that semicrystalline PMPO  formed  by  exposure to  methylethylketon vapor includes very  thin lamellae. The results are  consistent with  above-mentioned low Tm/Tg ratio in  this polymer.

It can  be  supposed  that crystalline particles of  different sizes can  be  formed depending of temperature and, hence,  the  rate  of solvent evaporation. If  the  size  of  these  crystallites is  less  than  some limiting value, WAXD scans  can appear  as  those of  a  completely amorphous  material. Indeed, Wenig et  al. [26]  showed  that semicrystalline PMPO  formed  by  exposure to  methylethylketon vapor includes very  thin lamellae. The results are  consistent with  above-mentioned low Tm/Tg ratio in  this polymer.
